cataloging_subject.label: The Works of William Herebert, OFM
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is Stephen R. Reimer's author-focused selection as represented by OTA 1213 and the U-M/General Corpus Herebert encoding: six Latin sermons, three Latin sermon outlines, and twenty-three Middle English poems. It is not one medieval work, not the whole multi-scribe miscellany British Library Add MS 46919, not every text written or annotated by Herebert, not Reimer's complete printed volume with introduction, apparatus, bibliography, indexes, and appendix sermon, and not the OTA, U-M, or repository serialization treated merely as a digital artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Works of William Herebert, OFM
catalog_summary.author: William Herebert, with disputed or unresolved attribution for Poems 7, 22, and 23 and Nicholas Bozon source responsibility for Poem 13
catalog_summary.date: Early fourteenth century, largely before 1333; Sermon 3 was preached 9 June 1314; source edition 1987; OTA deposit 1988
catalog_summary.region: Oxford preaching associations for Sermons 1, 3, and 4; South-West Midlands Middle English, localized to Herefordshire for the main nineteen-poem sequence

form_statements.description: The cataloged selection combines nine prose sermon items with twenty-three verse items.
Six sermons and three outlines are prose, although the sermons contain 73 line-like quotation elements.
The poem divisions contain 129 verse groups and 447 encoded line elements.

summaries.value: An author-focused collection of six Latin sermons, three Latin sermon outlines, and twenty-three Middle English religious and moral poems associated with the Franciscan William Herebert. The sermons display early-fourteenth-century Oxford Franciscan preaching and learning; the poems chiefly render hymns, antiphons, liturgical texts, Scripture, and Nicholas Bozon material into South-West Midlands Middle English. British Library Add MS 46919 preserves the entire selected corpus, much of it in Herebert's hand and with authorial revision.

The exact digital body contains thirty-two intellectual items: six sermons, three sermon outlines, and twenty-three poems. U-M organizes them in that order. OTA 1213 preserves the same items in the order twenty-three poems, six sermons, three outlines. The repository XML has eight DIV1 wrappers, twenty-six DIV2, two nested DIV3, 104 body P, 129 LG, 520 total L, 50 folio milestones, and 48 NOTE1; 447 L belong to poem divisions and 73 to quotations or line-like passages in sermons.

The printed Reimer volume also contains introduction, critical apparatus, commentary, bibliography, indexes, a plate of Add MS 46919 f. 171v, and an appendix seventh sermon in another hand. OTA, U-M, and General Corpus encode the selected work texts and authorial notes but omit the printed front matter and editorial notes; they do not encode the appendix sermon.

All checked source-witness evidence points to one physical codex, British Library Add MS 46919. DIMEV v1.0 identifies all twenty-three poems there; Poem 13 has two occurrences within that same codex, a seven-line trial draft at f. 84v and the final version at ff. 208v–209r. The digital text encodes the final version. Other manuscripts known to contain Herebert annotations are contextual, not evidenced witnesses of these selected texts.

OTA 1213 is a complete 1988 legacy text deposit using Chicago-guide-inspired inline codes. U-M converts those codes to structured XML, reorders the thirty-two items for navigation, promotes folio and occasion comments to milestones and headings, and moves authorial notes inline. Direct comparison confirms the same bounded contents but not byte identity; the precise conversion authority and several small character-level interventions remain undocumented.

extent.description: Thirty-two bounded intellectual items: six sermons, three sermon outlines, and twenty-three poems. The whole-item XML metric is 520 L elements: 447 occur within poem divisions and constitute the intrinsic poem-line extent, while 73 are embedded quotations or verse passages inside prose sermons and do not make those sermons verse works. Five header P are excluded from the 104 body P total. Encoded L counts are not normalized historical metrical-line counts.

content_parts.label: Six Latin sermons
content_parts.summary: Six sermons or sermon texts associated with William Herebert, including three explicitly tied to Oxford preaching contexts and one securely dated to 9 June 1314.

content_parts.label: Sermo 1: Dixit Mater Iesu ad eum, Vinum non habent
content_parts.summary: A Latin sermon for the second Sunday after Epiphany on John 2:3, headed as preached in the Church of the Blessed Virgin Mary at Oxford.
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.

content_parts.label: Sermo 2: Vidimus stellam eius in oriente
content_parts.summary: A Latin Epiphany sermon on Matthew 2:2, developing the Magi's coming, adoration, and the conditions of a good hearer.
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.

content_parts.label: Sermo 3: Homo quidam erat dives
content_parts.summary: A Latin sermon for the translation of St Edmund of Canterbury, preached in the Church of the Blessed Virgin Mary at Oxford on Sunday 9 June 1314.
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.

content_parts.label: Sermo 4: Laventur pedes vestri et requiescite
content_parts.summary: A Latin Maundy Thursday sermon headed as preached from the Oxford Franciscan pulpit.
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.

content_parts.label: Sermo 5: Homo cum in honore esset non intellexit
content_parts.summary: A Latin sermon of uncertain liturgical occasion on Psalm 48:13 in the Vulgate numbering.
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.

content_parts.label: Sermo 6: In carne sua stare fecit testamentum
content_parts.summary: A Latin sermon for the Circumcision of the Lord on Ecclesiasticus 44:21.
notes: The L count records line-like quotations embedded in a prose sermon; it is not a claim that the sermon is verse.

content_parts.label: Three Latin sermon outlines
content_parts.summary: Three shorter Latin sermon plans: one for St Luke and two for the Assumption of the Virgin.

content_parts.label: Sermon Outline 1: Quatuor facies uni
content_parts.summary: A Latin outline for the feast of St Luke, organized around Ezekiel's four faces.

content_parts.label: Sermon Outline 2: Non est mortua puella, sed dormit
content_parts.summary: A Latin outline for the Assumption of the Virgin on the girl who is not dead but asleep.

content_parts.label: Sermon Outline 3: Introduxit me rex in cellaria sua
content_parts.summary: A Latin outline for the Assumption of the Virgin on Song of Songs 1:3.

content_parts.label: Twenty-three Middle English poems
content_parts.summary: Twenty-three religious and moral poems, chiefly renderings or paraphrases of Latin hymns, antiphons, liturgical texts, and Scripture, with material connected to Nicholas Bozon.

locators.value: 23 DIMEV records; 24 witness occurrences because record-4895 has a trial draft and final version

notes: The 447 L elements are encoding units, not normalized metrical lines; DIMEV sometimes counts long manuscript or printed lines differently.
Poems 20–23 occur away from the continuous nineteen-poem sequence on ff. 205r–211v.

content_parts.label: Poem 1: Hostis Herodes impie
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Herodes, þou wykked fo”.

content_parts.label: Poem 2: Vexilla regis prodeunt
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Þe kynges baneres beth forth ylad”.

content_parts.label: Poem 3: Gloria, Laus, et Honor
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Wele, heriȝyng, and worshype boe to Crist”.

content_parts.label: Poem 4: Popule meus, quid feci tibi?
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “My volk, what habbe y do þe?”.

content_parts.label: Poem 5: Libera me, Domine
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Louerd, shyld me vrom helle deth”.

content_parts.label: Poem 6: Þou wommon boute uére
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Þou wommon boute uére”.
notes: Fifteen lines from the bottom margin are structurally nested in NOTE1 in U-M but remain part of the 57 encoded L total.

content_parts.label: Poem 7: Ave maris stella
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Hayl, Leuedy, se-stœrre bryht”.
notes: The current BL catalog excepts item vii from its general Herebert composition statement; DIMEV names Herebert. Attribution is therefore modeled as disputed.

content_parts.label: Poem 8: Veni Creator Spiritus
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Com, Shuppere, Holy Gost”.

content_parts.label: Poem 9: Alma redemptoris Mater
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Holy moder, þat bere Cryst”.

content_parts.label: Poem 10: Conditor alme siderum
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Holy wrouhte of sterres bryht”.

content_parts.label: Poem 11: Christe redemptor omnium
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Cryst, buggere of alle ycoren”.

content_parts.label: Poem 12: Tu rex glorie Christe
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Þou kyng of wœle and blisse”.

content_parts.label: Poem 13: Vous purueez en cete vye
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Bysœth ȝou in þysylke lyf”.
notes: DIMEV also records a seven-line trial draft on f. 84v after its Anglo-Norman source; the encoded poem follows the final version on ff. 208v–209r.

content_parts.label: Poem 14: Iesu nostra redemptio
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Iesu, oure raunsóun, loue, and lóngynge”.

content_parts.label: Poem 15: Eterne rex altissime
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Kyng hexst of alle kynges”.

content_parts.label: Poem 16: Quis est iste qui venit de Edom?
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “What ys he, þys lordling”.

content_parts.label: Poem 17: Crucem sanctam subiit
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “He sthey opon þe rode”.

content_parts.label: Poem 18: Audi benigne conditor
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Lustne, mylde Wrouhte”.

content_parts.label: Poem 19: Euangelium: Missus est angelus Gabriel
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Seynt Luk, in hys godspel”.
notes: The U-M encoding divides this poem into a prologue and the Missus est text using two nested DIV3 sections.

content_parts.label: Poem 20: Þys nome ys also on honikomb
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Þys nome ys also on honikomb”.

content_parts.label: Poem 21: Hic que uita?
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “What lyf ys þer her?”.

content_parts.label: Poem 22: Quomodo se habet homo?
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Also þe lanterne in þe wynd”.
notes: DIMEV describes the poem as perhaps by William Herebert.

content_parts.label: Poem 23: Vóur þynges ȝe ofte ysœth
content_parts.summary: Middle English verse beginning “Vóur þynges ȝe ofte ysœth”.
notes: DIMEV records the four lines in the lower margin of Herebert's translation of Nicholas Bozon's Contes moralisés; direct authorship remains unresolved.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_from_OTA/Herebert.xml, pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: HEADER, editorial declaration, and complete BODY
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for title, Reimer source, omitted print matter, U-M structure, headings, folio milestones, text, authorial notes, and exact selected contents.

evidence.citation: Direct lxml and element-count audit of the pinned Herebert XML.
evidence.summary: Confirms eight DIV1, twenty-six DIV2, two DIV3, 104 body P, 129 LG, 520 L, fifty folio milestones, forty-eight NOTE1, thirty-two intellectual items, 447 poem L, and 73 sermon-embedded L.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for Oxford Text Archive deposit 1213.
evidence.summary: Supports the 1988 deposit, source-edition identity, languages, license, file map, exact thirty-two-item payload, and legacy order.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for the current U-M CME Herebert item.
evidence.summary: Supports the electronic title, public item, current rights display, and U-M identity.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for Stephen R. Reimer's 1987 edition.
evidence.summary: Supports title, editor, imprint, series, extent, ISBN, copyright, contents, and poem pagination.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for British Library Add MS 46919.
evidence.summary: Supports the unique physical witness, exact sermon and poem loci, hands, date, material, extent, dimensions, Herebert associations, item-vii exception, and current access fields.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for DIMEV v1.0's twenty-three Herebert poem records.
evidence.summary: Supports poem identities, Reimer page references, BL Add 46919 loci, source relationships, subjects, attribution cautions, and the Poem 13 trial-draft/final-version distinction.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for Telfryn Pritchard's 1988 review.
evidence.summary: Supports the edition boundary, single manuscript basis, authorial revision, sermon and poem characterization, dialect, editorial policy, appendix exclusion, plate, bibliography, and indexes.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for the Manuscripts of the West Midlands Add MS 46919 record.
evidence.summary: Supports codicology, Herebert's hands, collation, and the Herefordshire dialect context of ff. 203–211.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for Christopher Fletcher's 2024 chapter on autograph literary manuscripts.
evidence.summary: Supports current scholarly context for autograph and lightly revised Herebert material in Add MS 46919 without assigning every codex item to him.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage audit of the OTA-to-U-M-to-repository transformation.
evidence.summary: Supports the shared thirty-two-item boundary, reordering, structural conversion, and non-byte-identical version relationship.

open_questions.question: How should current scholarship resolve the authorship of Poems 7, 22, and 23 and the exact Herebert/Bozon relationship for Poem 23?
next_steps: Consult Reimer's complete introduction and poem notes, Gneuss 1960, and current item-level scholarship.
Keep the BL item-vii exception and DIMEV's attribution as conflicting evidence until reconciled.
Do not infer Poem 23 authorship from its codex placement alone.

open_questions.question: What additional source identifications, dates, variants, and editorial qualifications appear in Reimer's inaccessible full introduction, notes, and apparatus?
next_steps: Purchase or consult the PIMS volume or an authorized library copy.
Compare each sermon and poem against Reimer's apparatus before strengthening item-level claims.
Inspect the appendix sermon only as excluded contextual material.

open_questions.question: Do any collateral manuscripts transmit versions of the six sermons, three outlines, or twenty-three poems beyond the one Add MS 46919 witness evidenced here?
next_steps: Search sermon repertories by incipit and source theme.
Check modern hymn and Middle English verse scholarship for lost, parallel, or derivative witnesses.
Do not treat other manuscripts annotated by Herebert as witnesses without item-level textual evidence.

open_questions.question: Who performed the U-M navigation transformation, when were the small textual interventions made, and what conversion specification governed them?
next_steps: Seek U-M or OTA processing documentation for the March 1996 edition.
Diff item-normalized OTA and U-M text at character level, separating structural movement from substantive variants.
Preserve both complete payloads and avoid describing U-M as a byte copy.

notes: The Reimer edition's appendix seventh sermon at Add MS 46919 ff. 163r–164v is outside the cataloged thirty-two-item unit even though it is physically adjacent to Herebert's sermons and shares f. 164v with two selected outlines.
Poems 20–23 are part of Reimer's twenty-three-poem edition but are physically discontiguous from the main nineteen-poem sequence.
The exact work-portion folios are f. 85r; ff. 159r–162v; f. 164v; ff. 165r–167v; ff. 169r–179v; ff. 181v–184v; f. 204v; and ff. 205r–211v.

A complete public facsimile of Add MS 46919 was not found as of 2026-07-15; that access finding is recorded in the lineage manifest and is not evidence that private, licensed, or later digitization does not exist.

Rights and access are layer-specific: medieval text, Reimer edition, OTA deposit, U-M presentation, repository artifact, DIMEV data, review images, and manuscript images are not assigned one blanket status.
